title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a food bank. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Leila, The Packing Rota Volunteer
Leila began helping at the food bank after her office donated boxes at Christmas and she realised the work continued long after the decorations disappeared. She was assigned to packing rotas. [47]The first problem was arranging access to the delivery bay, because the shutters were controlled by a shop next door that opened later than the volunteers arrived.[/47] On cold mornings, donated bread sat outside while everyone waited. [48]Her background in project management helped her create a rota that matched drivers, packers and dietary requests without leaving gaps.[/48] Volunteers joked that she treated tins of beans like a business launch, but the system reduced mistakes. Leila says the work taught her that dignity depends on details: a family should not have to explain twice that they cannot eat certain foods. She still feels nervous before busy sessions, yet she likes the quiet satisfaction of seeing labelled bags lined up correctly.
texts.B:
### Chris, The Cooking Demonstrator
[49]Chris was invited to run simple cooking demonstrations after the food bank manager tasted his soup at a neighbourhood lunch.[/49] He had trained as a chef, but cooking with donated ingredients was a different challenge. [50]He was surprised by the paperwork needed before visitors could use knives, portable hobs or even tasting spoons during the sessions.[/50] At first, he found the rules awkward and feared they would make the kitchen feel unfriendly. Then he saw how clear boundaries helped nervous participants relax. Chris focused on meals that used common items from the parcels, such as lentils, rice and tinned tomatoes. His proudest moment was when a man returned to say he had cooked the recipe for his children twice. Chris says the demonstrations are not about showing off skill; they are about turning a bag of unfamiliar ingredients into choices people can actually use. He now plans each session around confidence.
texts.C:
### Beth, The Shelf Sign Designer
Beth, a communications student, volunteered after waiting outside the food bank while her flatmate collected a parcel. [51]During those waits, she noticed people hesitating at the shelves because the handwritten notes were small, faded and partly hidden by stacked boxes.[/51] She offered to make the sorting area clearer. [55]Her new shelf signs used plain language and bold symbols so volunteers could identify vegetarian, baby, hygiene and allergy items quickly.[/55] [52]The most frustrating moment came when a leaking bottle of shampoo soaked the first complete set of labels overnight.[/52] Beth wanted to cry, but the next morning two volunteers helped her reprint everything and seal the signs in plastic. She says the project showed her that communication is not only public campaigns or clever slogans. Sometimes it is a label that prevents someone receiving the wrong food, or helps a tired volunteer keep moving calmly. Those small improvements mattered more than praise.
texts.D:
### Aaron, The Delivery Route Helper
[53]Aaron became involved because his grandmother had once relied on food parcels after a long illness, and he remembered how much discreet deliveries mattered to her.[/53] He started by carrying boxes, then helped plan routes for households unable to collect parcels. [54]His hardest task was scheduling deliveries so drivers could unload quietly near flats without blocking entrances, upsetting neighbours or drawing attention to recipients.[/54] He learned that speed was not always kindness. Some people preferred a slower delivery at a time when the hallway was empty. Aaron was moved when one elderly man left a thank-you note taped inside a returned crate. [56]He is now making route notes that other drivers can follow, so the service does not depend on his knowledge of every doorway and parking space.[/56] He says privacy is part of generosity, not an optional extra. That belief guides every route. He carries that lesson carefully now.

questions.question: mentions a delivery access problem caused by another business opening later?
questions.explanation: Leila says the delivery bay shutters were controlled by a shop that opened later than the volunteers arrived.

questions.question: used project-management experience to organise people and individual requirements?
questions.explanation: Leila matched drivers, packers and dietary requests in a rota.

questions.question: was invited after the organiser tasted something they had made?
questions.explanation: Chris was invited after the food bank manager tasted his soup.

questions.question: was surprised by safety paperwork before people could use cooking equipment?
questions.explanation: Chris was surprised by forms for knives, portable hobs and tasting spoons.

questions.question: noticed people struggling with unclear written information while waiting outside?
questions.explanation: Beth noticed people hesitating at shelves because the notes were small, faded and hidden.

questions.question: had to replace a completed set of materials after an accidental leak?
questions.explanation: A leaking shampoo bottle soaked Beth’s first set of labels.

questions.question: joined because of a family memory of receiving this kind of support?
questions.explanation: Aaron remembered his grandmother relying on food parcels after illness.

questions.question: had to schedule deliveries to protect people’s privacy and avoid causing disruption?
questions.explanation: Aaron scheduled deliveries to avoid blocking entrances, upsetting neighbours or drawing attention.

questions.question: made signs that helped volunteers identify different categories quickly?
questions.explanation: Beth made shelf signs for vegetarian, baby, hygiene and allergy items.

questions.question: is creating route notes so deliveries do not depend on their personal knowledge?
questions.explanation: Aaron is making route notes for other drivers.
